The text starts by talking about humans considering themselves superior and living in the information age but not knowing everything. Then it gives examples of disappearances in the Bermuda Triangle and mentions that even Columbus had issues there. The author lists various theories about the Bermuda Triangle but says the mystery remains unsolved. The conclusion suggests maybe we need to wait until Heaven to get the answer. So, the author uses the Bermuda Triangle as an example to make a point about human limitations in knowledge. The first paragraph mentions human vanity and the belief that we must know everything, but we can't. The Bermuda Triangle is presented as proof that there are things we don't know. Looking at the options: A) Tell some stories about the Bermuda Triangle. The text does include some stories, but is that the main purpose? The text doesn't really mention anything about warnings or avoiding the area. It's more about the mystery than danger. C) Laugh at man's vanity to know everything. The text does mention human vanity and our inability to know everything, but is it to "laugh at"? The tone is more reflective than mocking. D) Prove man does not have the ability to know all.
